medicaid.name: Medicaid
medicaid.description: Government health coverage for low-income individuals and families
eligibility_rules: Income must be at or below 138% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL)
Must be a U.S. citizen or qualifying immigrant
Must be a resident of the state where applying
Covers children, pregnant women, adults, elderly, and people with disabilities
In expansion states: adults 19-64 earning up to 138% FPL qualify
In non-expansion states: eligibility may be more restricted for adults without children
medicaid.how_to_apply: Apply through your state's Medicaid agency or at healthcare.gov

aca_marketplace.name: ACA Health Insurance Marketplace
aca_marketplace.description: Subsidized private insurance plans for people who don't qualify for Medicaid but need affordable coverage
aca_marketplace.federal_poverty_level_range: 100% to 400% FPL (subsidies available; some states extended to higher incomes)
eligibility_rules: Must be a U.S. citizen or qualifying immigrant
Must not have access to affordable employer-sponsored insurance
Income between 100% and 400% FPL qualifies for premium tax credits
Income above 400% FPL can still purchase marketplace plans (no subsidy)
Must not be eligible for Medicaid or Medicare
Open enrollment is typically November 1 – January 15
Special enrollment periods available for life events (job loss, marriage, birth of child)
subsidy_types: Premium Tax Credit (PTC): reduces monthly premium cost
Cost-Sharing Reduction (CSR): lowers deductibles and copays (only on Silver plans)
aca_marketplace.how_to_apply: Apply at healthcare.gov during open enrollment or a special enrollment period

chip.name: Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP)
chip.description: Low-cost health coverage for children in families that earn too much for Medicaid but cannot afford private insurance
chip.federal_poverty_level_threshold: Up to 200% FPL in most states (some states go higher)
eligibility_rules: Child must be under 19 years old
Family income is too high for Medicaid but cannot afford private insurance
Most states cover children up to 200% FPL; some up to 300% or higher
Child must be a U.S. citizen or qualifying immigrant
Pregnant women may also qualify for CHIP in some states

income_examples.family_of_4: Roughly $60,000/year or less in most states (varies by state)

chip.how_to_apply: Apply through your state's CHIP program or at insurekidsnow.gov

federal_poverty_levels_2024.note: These are continental U.S. figures. Alaska and Hawaii have higher thresholds.

common_questions: What is the income limit for Medicaid?
How do I get health insurance if I lost my job?
Can my child get CHIP coverage?
What is the difference between Medicaid and the ACA?
I'm pregnant — what health coverage do I qualify for?
